title: Maintenance & Alimony After Divorce Bangladesh – Wife's Legal Rights Explained
metaTitle: Maintenance After Divorce Bangladesh – Wife's Alimony Rights & How to Claim
metaDescription: What maintenance can a wife claim after divorce Bangladesh? Complete guide to iddat maintenance, dower, long-term alimony, Family Court process, and how to enforce your financial rights after divorce.
keywords: wife financial rights after divorce Bangladesh
heroIntro: After divorce, a wife in Bangladesh has specific financial rights that the husband is legally required to fulfil. Many women do not know what they are legally entitled to claim — or how to enforce those rights through the Family Court. This guide explains every financial right a divorced wife has under Bangladesh law.
toc: Overview of Financial Rights After Divorce in Bangladesh
Long-Term Maintenance: Is It Available in Bangladesh?
Child Maintenance: How It Relates to Wife's Rights
How to File a Maintenance Case in Family Court
What Happens If the Ex-Husband Refuses to Pay?

sections.h2: Overview of Financial Rights After Divorce in Bangladesh
sections.content:
A divorced wife in Bangladesh has three primary financial entitlements:

  1. Iddat maintenance (nafaqa): Financial support during the waiting period after divorce — typically 3 months
  2. Unpaid dower (mahr/denmohar): The outstanding balance of the bride price specified in the Kabin Nama
  3. Child maintenance (nafaqa al-awlad): Ongoing financial support for children — this is technically the children's right, not the wife's, but the custodial mother receives and manages it

Under classical Islamic law (applied in Bangladesh as personal law for Muslims), there is no concept of indefinite Western-style alimony after divorce. The husband's financial obligations to a divorced wife end after the iddat period and after all dower is paid. However, if children are in the mother's custody, the husband must continue supporting the children financially.

A divorce lawyer in Bangladesh will assess your specific situation and ensure you claim every financial entitlement you are legally owed.

Iddah (also spelled iddat) is the waiting period that a Muslim woman must observe after divorce before she can remarry. During this period, the ex-husband is legally obligated to provide maintenance (nafaqa) to the divorced wife.

Duration of iddah:

  • Standard divorce: 3 menstrual cycles (approximately 3 months)
  • Post-menopausal wife: 3 lunar months
  • Pregnant wife: Until delivery of the child

What iddah maintenance covers:

  • Housing (the wife is entitled to remain in the marital home during iddah, or receive equivalent housing allowance)
  • Food and basic sustenance
  • Medical expenses

Important: The obligation to pay iddah maintenance exists regardless of the reason for divorce — whether it was talaq by the husband, Khul by the wife, or court decree. Even in Khul, the wife retains her iddah maintenance rights.

The amount of iddah maintenance is based on the husband's means and the wife's reasonable standard of living. If the husband refuses to pay, the wife can file a maintenance case in the Family Court under the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 and the Family Courts Ordinance 1985.

Mahr (or denmohor in Bengali) is the sum of money or property that the husband promises to pay the wife at the time of marriage. It is specified in the Kabin Nama (Muslim marriage contract). Mahr has two components:

  • Prompt (muajjal) mahr: Payable on demand or at marriage. If not paid, the wife has the right to refuse cohabitation until it is paid.
  • Deferred (muazzal) mahr: Payable upon divorce or death of the husband.

Upon divorce, any unpaid or outstanding mahr (prompt or deferred) becomes immediately payable. This is an absolute debt owed by the husband (or his estate) to the wife — it cannot be waived by him unilaterally.

Claiming unpaid mahr:

  • The wife can file a suit for recovery of mahr/dower in the Family Court
  • The suit is based on a contractual obligation — the Kabin Nama is the primary evidence
  • If the mahr amount is disputed, the court will look at the registered Kabin Nama as authoritative evidence
  • An unregistered Kabin Nama or an oral marriage still creates mahr rights — other evidence of the agreed amount can be produced

Never waive your mahr rights without careful legal advice from a divorce lawyer in Bangladesh. In some Khul negotiations, women are pressured to waive mahr entirely — this may not be necessary or legally fair.

sections.h2: Long-Term Maintenance: Is It Available in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
Under classical Islamic law (applied to Muslims in Bangladesh), there is generally no right to indefinite post-divorce maintenance beyond the iddah period. Once iddah is complete and dower is paid, the husband's financial obligations to the divorced wife cease.

However, there are important exceptions:

  • Pregnant wife: Maintenance continues until childbirth and for the nursing period thereafter.
  • Custodial parent of minor children: The mother caring for the husband's children receives child maintenance — which, while technically the children's right, comes to the mother as their custodian.
  • Disabled wife: Where a wife is unable to support herself due to disability, courts have made orders for extended maintenance on humanitarian grounds.
  • Contractual maintenance: A separation agreement or divorce settlement can include agreed maintenance payments for any period the parties choose to agree on.

In a contested divorce, negotiating a good financial settlement — covering dower, iddah maintenance, and child support — is where skilled legal representation makes the biggest difference. Contact Adv. Shah Alam to protect your financial rights.

A wife does not have to wait until divorce to claim maintenance. If the husband has abandoned the wife, failed to maintain her, or the parties are separated, the wife can file a maintenance case in the Family Court while still married.

Under Section 3 of the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 and the Family Courts Ordinance 1985, the Family Court has jurisdiction to order a husband to pay maintenance to his wife. A wife filing for maintenance during separation must establish:

  • That she is a lawfully married wife
  • That the husband has failed to maintain her without reasonable cause
  • The appropriate amount of maintenance based on the husband's means

Courts can grant interim maintenance orders quickly — sometimes at the very first hearing — to ensure the wife is not left destitute while the main case is pending.

sections.h2: How to File a Maintenance Case in Family Court
sections.content:
Filing a maintenance case in the Bangladesh Family Court involves the following steps:

  1. Engage a family lawyer: A divorce and family lawyer will review your Kabin Nama, marriage certificate, and financial situation to prepare your case.
  2. Determine the correct court: Maintenance cases are filed in the Family Court of the district where the wife or husband resides.
  3. File the petition: A formal petition specifying the grounds for maintenance, the amount claimed, and the husband's means is filed with the court. Supporting documents (Kabin Nama, marriage certificate, children's birth certificates, evidence of husband's income) are attached.
  4. Court summons: The court issues a summons requiring the husband to appear and respond.
  5. Mediation: The Family Court is legally required to attempt conciliation/mediation between the parties.
  6. Hearing: Both parties present evidence and arguments.
  7. Maintenance order: The court makes an order specifying the monthly maintenance amount, payable from a specified date.

A woman facing financial hardship does not have to wait months for the final maintenance order. The Family Court has the power to grant interim maintenance at any stage of the proceedings — and specifically at the first hearing.

To obtain interim maintenance:

  • Your lawyer files an application for interim maintenance simultaneously with or soon after the main petition
  • The court considers the application at the next hearing
  • If satisfied of prima facie entitlement, the court orders the husband to pay a specified amount immediately while the main case continues

Interim maintenance orders can be enforced as soon as they are passed — failure to comply makes the husband liable for contempt of court. This is a powerful tool to immediately secure your financial position.

A maintenance order from the Family Court is legally binding. If the ex-husband fails to comply with a maintenance order, enforcement options include:

  • Execution proceedings: The wife files an execution petition with the court. The court can attach the husband's bank accounts, salary, or movable property to recover the unpaid amount.
  • Contempt of court: Wilful failure to obey a court maintenance order is contempt — the husband can be summoned and fined or imprisoned for contempt.
  • Warrant for arrest: In persistent default cases, courts can issue warrants for the arrest of the defaulting husband.
  • Property attachment: The court can attach and sell the husband's movable or immovable property to satisfy unpaid maintenance arrears.

Do not tolerate a husband who ignores a maintenance order — your lawyer can immediately file for enforcement. Contact Adv. Shah Alam to enforce a maintenance order that is being ignored.

sections.h2: What Happens If the Ex-Husband Refuses to Pay?
sections.content:
If the ex-husband refuses to pay iddah maintenance, dower, or a court-ordered maintenance, you have powerful remedies:

  • File an execution case: The Family Court's execution jurisdiction allows property attachment and even imprisonment for debt.
  • Criminal complaint: Section 488 of the CrPC allows a criminal complaint against a husband who refuses to maintain his wife or children — this is a separate route from the civil maintenance case and can result in imprisonment.
  • Contempt of court: If a specific court order is being disobeyed, contempt proceedings are fast and effective.
  • Attachment of earnings: Courts can order an employer to deduct maintenance from the husband's salary before it reaches him.

A divorced wife in Bangladesh is never without legal remedy if her ex-husband defaults on his financial obligations. The courts are fully equipped to enforce these rights — but you need an experienced lawyer to invoke them effectively.

faqs.question: Does a wife have to prove hardship to claim maintenance after divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: For iddah maintenance and dower, no — these are absolute rights that do not depend on proving financial hardship. For maintenance during marriage (pre-divorce), the wife must show the husband has failed to provide without reasonable cause. The amount is based on the husband's means, not just the wife's need.

faqs.question: Can a working wife claim maintenance after divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. A wife's employment does not automatically disentitle her to maintenance — particularly iddah maintenance and unpaid dower, which are absolute rights. For ongoing maintenance during marriage, the husband's obligation exists regardless of whether the wife works.

faqs.question: How is the maintenance amount determined by the Family Court?
faqs.answer: The court considers the husband's income, assets, and means; the wife's own financial resources; the standard of living during the marriage; the number and needs of dependent children; and any other relevant circumstances. There is no fixed formula — the amount is based on what is fair and reasonable in the specific case.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get a maintenance order in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: An interim maintenance order can sometimes be obtained at the first or second hearing — within weeks of filing. A final maintenance order typically takes 6–18 months depending on whether the husband contests and the court's workload.

faqs.question: Can a wife claim maintenance if she has remarried?
faqs.answer: No. A divorced wife's right to maintenance from her ex-husband ceases when she remarries. Child maintenance obligations, however, continue regardless of the mother's remarriage.
